'''Jody David Scheckter''' ({{IPAc-en|ˈ|ʃ|ɛ|k|t|ər}}; born 29 January 1950) is a South African former [[racing driver]] and businessman who competed in [[Formula One]] from {{F1|1972}} to {{F1|1980}}. Scheckter won the [[Formula One World Drivers' Championship]] in {{F1|1979}} with [[Scuderia Ferrari|Ferrari]], and remains the only African driver to have [[List of Formula One Grand Prix winners|won a Formula One Grand Prix]] or the World Drivers' Championship; he won 10 Grands Prix across nine seasons.

Born and raised in [[East London, Cape Province]], Scheckter rapidly ascended through the ranks of [[motor racing]] upon moving to the United Kingdom in 1971. His Formula One debut came the following year at the {{F1GP|1972|United States}}, driving for [[McLaren]], who he had raced for that year in [[British Formula Two|British]] and [[1972 European Formula Two season|European Formula Two]]. Amongst winning the [[1973 SCCA L&M Championship|SCCA Continental Championship]] in [[Formula 5000]], Scheckter entered a further five Grands Prix in {{F1|1973}} with McLaren. Scheckter earned a full-time drive with [[Tyrrell Racing|Tyrrell]] the [[1974 Formula One season|following season]], taking his maiden win at the {{F1GP|1974|Swedish}} and finishing third in the championship. Scheckter won [[1975 South African Grand Prix|his home Grand Prix in South Africa]] in {{F1|1975}} and placed third in the standings again in {{F1|1976}}, driving the six-wheeled [[Tyrrell P34|P34]]; he remains the only driver to [[1976 Swedish Grand Prix|win a Grand Prix]] on six wheels.

Moving to [[Walter Wolf Racing|Wolf]] in {{F1|1977}}, Scheckter took several race wins—including the {{F1GP|1977|Monaco}}—as he finished runner-up to [[Niki Lauda]] in the standings. After a winless season for Wolf in {{F1|1978}}, Scheckter moved to [[Scuderia Ferrari|Ferrari]] to partner [[Gilles Villeneuve]]. Winning three Grands Prix in [[1979 Formula One season|his first season]] for Ferrari, Scheckter clinched the title with his victory at the {{F1GP|1979|Italian}}, becoming the first World Drivers' Champion from Africa. He was unable to defend his title in {{F1|1980}} as Ferrari struggled with the [[Ferrari 312T#312T5|312T5]], retiring at the end of the season having achieved 10 race wins, three pole positions, five fastest laps and 33 podiums in Formula One.

Upon retiring from motor racing, Scheckter worked as a [[sports broadcasting|broadcaster]] for [[CBS Sports|CBS]], [[ESPN on ABC|ABC]] and [[ITV (TV network)|ITV]].  He founded a [[Military simulation|weapons simulation]] business in 1984 called ''Firearms Training Systems'', whose [[revenue]] eclipsed £100 million by the early 1990s. Scheckter then moved into [[organic farming]], owning [[Laverstoke Park Farm]] in England. His sons [[Toby Scheckter|Toby]] and [[Tomas Scheckter|Tomas]] are both racing drivers.

==Career==

Jody David Scheckter was born on 29 January 1950 in [[East London, South Africa|East London]], [[Cape Province]], [[Union of South Africa]],<ref name=":0" /> and educated at Vincent Primary School (East London) and [[Port Rex Technical High School]].{{citation needed|date=September 2024}}

===Formula One===
[[File:Scheckter Britain 1974.jpg|thumb|left|Scheckter in a [[Tyrrell 007]] at the [[1974 British Grand Prix]] at [[Brands Hatch]].]]

==== 1972-73: Debut years ====
Scheckter rapidly ascended to the ranks of Formula One after moving to Britain in early 1971. His Formula 1 debut occurred at the US Grand Prix at [[Watkins Glen International|Watkins Glen]] in [[1972 Formula One season|1972]] with [[Team McLaren|McLaren]], where he ran as high as third place before spinning and finishing ninth. Immediately becoming a name to watch, he continued his development the following year, winning the [[1973 SCCA L&M Championship]] and racing five times in F1.  In France, he almost won in only his third start in F1 before crashing into [[Emerson Fittipaldi]], the reigning World Champion, who said after the crash about Scheckter: "This madman is a menace to himself and everybody else and does not belong in Formula 1."<ref name="hof"/> In his next start, the British Grand Prix at [[Silverstone Circuit|Silverstone]], Scheckter's spin triggered a major accident which took nearly a dozen cars out of the race.  The [[Grand Prix Drivers Association]] demanded his immediate banishment, which was only put off when McLaren agreed to rest their driver for four races.<ref name="hof"/> Scheckter's [[McLaren M23]] bore the number zero during the Canadian and American Grands Prix of 1973.  Scheckter is one of only two F1 drivers to compete under this number, the other being [[Damon Hill]]. During the practice for the American event at the [[Watkins Glen International|Watkins Glen]] circuit, Frenchman [[François Cevert]], who was due to be Scheckter's Tyrrell teammate for 1974, was killed in an appalling accident at the fast uphill Esses corners. Scheckter was behind Cevert when he crashed, and he stopped his McLaren, got out of his car and attempted to help Cevert out of his destroyed Tyrrell, but the 29-year-old Frenchman had been cut in half by the circuit's poorly installed Armco barriers and was already dead. Witnessing Cevert's dreadful accident left an indelible mark on the South African and caused him to abandon his reckless ways, becoming a more mature and calculating driver as a result.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Zincenko |first1=Roman |title=Formula 1 champion Jody Scheckter on life after the fast lane |url=https://www.elitelivingafrica.com/motoring/formula-1-champion-jody-scheckter-on-life-after-the-fast-lane |website=Elite Living Africa |publisher=Alain Charles Publishing Ltd. |access-date=2 December 2021 |date=2020}}</ref>

==== 1974-76: Tyrrell years ====
[[File:ScheckterJody1976-07-31Tyrrell-FordP34.jpg|thumb|Scheckter in the iconic six-wheel [[Tyrrell P34]] at the [[Nürburgring]] in 1976.]]
[[Tyrrell Racing|Tyrrell]] gave Scheckter his first full-time drive in F1, signing him for the [[1974 Formula One season|1974 season]]. After failing to score from the first three rounds with the [[Tyrrell 006|006]] model, a switch to the [[Tyrrell 007|007 chassis]] rewarded Scheckter with his first points finish at the [[1974 Spanish Grand Prix|Spanish Grand Prix]].<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.formula1.com/results/season/1974/475/ |title=1974 Spanish Grand Prix |publisher=formula1.com |access-date=22 December 2015 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150118203435/http://www.formula1.com/results/season/1974/475/ |archive-date=18 January 2015}}</ref> Despite a collision with [[Tom Pryce]], Scheckter finished third in [[1974 Belgian Grand Prix|Belgium]], thus scoring his maiden F1 podium.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Jenkinson |first=Denis |date=2014-07-07 |title=1974 Belgian Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/june-1974/29/the-grand-prix-in-belgium/ |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB}}</ref> Having finished second in [[1974 Monaco Grand Prix|Monaco]], Scheckter passed polesitting teammate [[Patrick Depailler]] at the start of the [[1974 Swedish Grand Prix|Swedish Grand Prix]] and held on to win the race.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Jenkinson |first=Denis |date=2014-07-07 |title=1974 Monaco Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/july-1974/45/the-32nd-monaco-grand-prix/ |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr242.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> He scored top fives in the next two events, before benefiting from a puncture for [[Niki Lauda|Niki Lauda's]] [[Scuderia Ferrari|Ferrari]] to win in [[1974 British Grand Prix|Britain]] and take over the championship lead.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Henry |first=Alan |date=2014-07-07 |title=1974 British Grand Prix - Scheckter finishes first |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/september-1974/47/the-british-grand-prix-19/ |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB}}</ref> Scheckter finished second in [[1974 German Grand Prix|Germany]] but retired early in [[1974 Austrian Grand Prix|Austria]] with a blown engine.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr246.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr247.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> Scheckter profited from reliability woes for the two Ferraris to finish third in [[1974 Italian Grand Prix|Italy]], a result that took him to within a point of standings leader [[Clay Regazzoni]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr248.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> His championship challenge suffered a bitter blow in [[1974 Canadian Grand Prix|Canada]]; running third, Scheckter crashed after the race's halfway mark due to a brake failure.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr249.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> Though he was still eligible to win the title at the season finale at [[1974 United States Grand Prix|Watkins Glen]], Scheckter retired with a fuel pressure problem while holding off eventual champion [[Emerson Fittipaldi]] in fourth place.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Henry |first=Alan |date=2014-07-07 |title=1974 United States Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/november-1974/21/the-united-states-grand-prix/ |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB}}</ref> Scheckter finished third in the championship.<ref>{{Cite web |title=F1 - The Official Home of Formula 1® Racing |url=https://www.formula1.com/ |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=Formula 1® - The Official F1® Website |language=en}}</ref>

Scheckter's [[1975 Formula One season|1975]] began with two non-scores; he finished 11th in [[1975 Argentine Grand Prix|Argentina]] after colliding with [[Jochen Mass]] at the start, then retired in [[1975 Brazilian Grand Prix|Brazil]] with an oil tank issue.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr251.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr252.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> At his home race in [[1975 South African Grand Prix|South Africa]], Scheckter won in emphatic fashion, passing [[Carlos Pace]] for the lead on lap 3 and leading every lap from thereon.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr253.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=MacLeman |first=Greg |date=18 March 2024 |title=Jody Scheckter's Top Five Most Memorable Formula 1 Moments |url=https://rmsothebys.com/all-stories-videos/jody-scheckter-s-top-five-most-memorable-formula-1-moments/ |access-date=28 December 2025 |website=RM Sotheby's}}</ref> In [[1975 Spanish Grand Prix|Spain]], Scheckter suffered an early engine failure, whilst a puncture demoted him from a points position in [[1975 Monaco Grand Prix|Monaco]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr254.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/races/monaco-gp-1975.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> Scheckter worked his way to second in [[1975 Belgian Grand Prix|Belgium]], though he failed to score points in the subsequent three races.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/races/belgian-gp-1975.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> This included a strong run at [[1975 Dutch Grand Prix|Zandvoort]], where a potential third place was scuppered by a late engine failure.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr258.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> During the final laps of a weather-affected [[1975 British Grand Prix|British Grand Prix]], Scheckter went off, but was nevertheless credited with third place as the race was [[Red Flagged F1 Race|red flagged]] and the countback rule was applied.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr260.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> A horrible race followed at the [[1975 German Grand Prix|Nürburgring]], as Scheckter dropped from third to 20th at the start and later crashed out due to a tire failure.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Jenkinson |first=Denis |date=2014-07-07 |title=1975 German Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/september-1975/26/the-german-grand-prix-reality/ |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr261.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> Eighth places in [[1975 Austrian Grand Prix|Austria]] and [[1975 Italian Grand Prix|Italy]], where Scheckter spun from third on lap 2, preceded a final points score with sixth in the [[1975 United States Grand Prix|United States]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr263.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/races/united-states-gp-1975.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> With just four points finishes across the season, Scheckter dropped to seventh in the standings.<ref>{{Cite web |title=F1 - The Official Home of Formula 1® Racing |url=https://www.formula1.com/ |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=Formula 1® - The Official F1® Website |language=en}}</ref>

Having started [[1976 Formula One season|1976]] with two points finishes from the opening four races, Scheckter and teammate Depailler switched to Tyrrell's radical, six-wheeled [[Tyrrell P34]]. After finishing fourth in [[1976 Belgian Grand Prix|Belgium]], Scheckter gave the six-wheeler its maiden podium in [[1976 Monaco Grand Prix|Monaco]] (a second place), as well as its only win at the [[1976 Swedish Grand Prix|Swedish Grand Prix]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr270.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Jenkinson |first=Denis |date=2014-07-07 |title=1976 Swedish Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/july-1976/23/swedish-grand-prix-4/ |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB}}</ref> He then finished second in two successive races in [[1976 British Grand Prix|Britain]] and [[1976 German Grand Prix|Germany]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr273.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr274.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> A crash caused by a broken suspension ended his race in [[1976 Austrian Grand Prix|Austria]], though Scheckter returned to the top five in the next three events.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr275.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> At the [[1976 United States Grand Prix|United States Grand Prix]], Scheckter battled [[James Hunt]] for the victory all race, but eventually came up short and took second.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr279.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Henry |first=Alan |date=2015-09-02 |title=1976 United States Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/november-1976/23/united-states-grand-prix-3/ |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB}}</ref> Despite retiring with overheating problems in [[1976 Japanese Grand Prix|Japan]], Scheckter had done enough to end the year third overall.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr280.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> Despite scoring points in ten of the 12 races he contested with the P34, Scheckter later went on record as saying the car was "a piece of junk" and left the team after 1976.<ref>{{cite web |last=Medland |first=Chris |title=The winning 'piece of junk' |url=http://en.espnf1.com/f1/motorsport/story/68184.html |access-date=13 March 2013 |publisher=ESPN}}</ref>

==== 1977-78: Walter Wolf Racing ====
Scheckter joined the new [[Walter Wolf Racing]] team in [[1977 Formula One season|1977]]. At its maiden race in [[1977 Argentine Grand Prix|Argentina]], Scheckter benefited from reliability issues for his rivals to claim victory.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr281.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> Engine issues stopped his race early in [[1977 Brazilian Grand Prix|Brazil]], though Scheckter bounced back by taking second in [[1977 South African Grand Prix|South Africa]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/races/brazilian-gp-1977.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Jenkinson |first=Denis |date=2014-07-07 |title=1977 South African Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/april-1977/56/south-african-grand-prix-12/ |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB}}</ref> Scheckter took the lead at the start of the [[1977 United States Grand Prix West|United States Grand Prix West]], but was passed by [[Mario Andretti]] in the closing stages, having felt a deflating front right tyre; he nevertheless finished third.<ref>{{Cite web |date=28 October 2014 |title=Long Beach 77 - Andretti's unique home victory |url=https://www.formula1.com/en/latest/article/long-beach-77-andrettis-unique-home-victory.7eQqWwYfyiUhKAvBQ6bpAk |access-date=29 December 2025 |website=Formula 1}}</ref> Third in [[1977 Spanish Grand Prix|Spain]] preceded a win in [[1977 Monaco Grand Prix|Monaco]], where Scheckter passed [[John Watson (racing driver)|John Watson]] off the line and led every lap.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/races/spanish-gp-1977.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Jenkinson |first=Denis |date=2015-11-24 |title=1977 Monaco Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/july-1977/61/monaco-grand-prix-9/ |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB}}</ref> Scheckter led the [[1977 Belgian Grand Prix|Belgian Grand Prix]] early on, but fell back after pitting for dry tyres later than his rivals on the drying track. He later retired with engine problems.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr287.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> In [[1977 Swedish Grand Prix|Sweden]], Scheckter retired after colliding with second-placed Watson in an attempt to overtake.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr288.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> He then crashed out on his own in [[1977 French Grand Prix|France]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr289.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> Scheckter's fourth successive retirement came in [[1977 British Grand Prix|Britain]], where an engine failure took him out of third place.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr290.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> Scheckter took pole for the [[1977 German Grand Prix|German Grand Prix]], and finished second after losing out to [[Niki Lauda]] in a battle for the lead.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr291.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> At the following race in [[1977 Austrian Grand Prix|Austria]], Scheckter spun out, thus handing Lauda a 16-point championship advantage.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Jenkinson |first=Denis |date=2015-04-01 |title=1977 Austrian Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/september-1977/25/austrian-grand-prix-7/ |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/races/austrian-gp-1977.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> Lauda extended the gap to 21 points with a win in the [[1977 Dutch Grand Prix|Netherlands]], while Scheckter inherited third after [[Patrick Tambay]] ran out of fuel on the penultimate lap.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr293.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> Another blow to Scheckter's title chances came in [[1977 Italian Grand Prix|Monza]], the South African retiring from second place with another engine failure.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/races/italian-gp-1977.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> Even though Scheckter finished third at [[1977 United States Grand Prix|Watkins Glen]], Lauda had done enough to clinch the title two races in advance.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/races/united-states-gp-1977-2.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> Luck found Scheckter at the penultimate race in [[1977 Canadian Grand Prix|Canada]], where an engine failure for the dominant Andretti handed him and Wolf their third win of the season.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Latest Formula 1 Breaking News - Grandprix.com |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr296.html |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> Scheckter concluded the year by finishing tenth in [[1977 Japanese Grand Prix|Japan]]; he finished second in the championship thanks to nine podiums.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Formula 1 real all time points comparison |url=https://www.formula1points.com/ |access-date=2025-12-28 |website=Formula 1 real all time points |language=english}}</ref>

By the start of the [[1978 Formula One season|1978 season]], [[Walter Wolf|Walter Wolf's]] race team had become less competitive, the car having to be rebuilt after finishing a lowly tenth in [[1978 Argentine Grand Prix|Argentina]].<ref name=":1">{{Cite web |last=admin |date=2014-07-07 |title=1978 Brazilian Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/march-1978/76/the-brazilian-grand-prix-goes-to-rio/ |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB}}</ref> Scheckter retired from the next three races: he bent his suspension after colliding with Patrick Tambay in [[1978 Brazilian Grand Prix|Brazil]].<ref name=":1" /> He then spun out of his [[1978 South African Grand Prix|home Grand Prix]] after sliding with low engine power caused by a fuel injection pressure drop-off, having previously led the race before falling to sixth.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Jenkinson |first=Denis |date=2014-07-07 |title=1978 South African Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/april-1978/46/south-african-grand-prix-15/ |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB}}</ref> Finally, Scheckter collided with Tambay while defending fifth place at [[1978 United States Grand Prix West|Long Beach]].<ref>{{Cite web |last=Jenkinson |first=Denis |date=2014-07-07 |title=1978 United States Grand Prix West race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/may-1978/84/the-united-states-grand-prix-west-2/ |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB}}</ref> Scheckter collected his first points with a third place finish at [[1978 Monaco Grand Prix|Monaco]], where he nursed the car home despite breaking second gear with three laps to go.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Jenkinson |first=Denis |date=2014-07-07 |title=1978 Monaco Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/june-1978/36/monaco-grand-prix-12/ |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB}}</ref> In [[1978 Belgian Grand Prix|Belgium]], startline contact with Lauda led to Scheckter having to retire later on.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Belgian GP, 1978 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr303.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> Using the new [[Wolf WR5]], Scheckter finished fourth in [[1978 Spanish Grand Prix|Spain]] but retired with water pipe issues in [[1978 Swedish Grand Prix|Sweden]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Spanish GP, 1978 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr304.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Swedish GP, 1978 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr305.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> He took sixth in [[1978 French Grand Prix|France]], then led the [[1978 British Grand Prix|British Grand Prix]] before developing a gearbox issue which dropped him out.<ref>{{Cite web |title=British GP, 1978 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr307.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> Scheckter qualified fourth in [[1978 German Grand Prix|Germany]] but fell to the back with fuel-feed problems; he battled back to finish a strong second.<ref>{{Cite web |title=German GP, 1978 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr308.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> In the heavy rain at [[1978 Austrian Grand Prix|Spielberg]], Scheckter crashed out on lap 4, having previously run second.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Austrian GP, 1978 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr309.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> After finishing 12th at [[1978 Dutch Grand Prix|Zandvoort]] and [[1978 Italian Grand Prix|Monza]], Scheckter claimed third at the [[1978 United States Grand Prix|United States Grand Prix]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=United States GP, 1978 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr312.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> Scheckter concluded the season in strong fashion, qualifying second in [[1978 Canadian Grand Prix|Canada]] and, after being passed by a fast [[Gilles Villeneuve]], finishing second.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Canadian GP, 1978 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr313.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> With seventh place in the championship, Scheckter left Wolf to join Villeneuve at Ferrari.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Formula 1 real all time points comparison |url=https://www.formula1points.com/ |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=Formula 1 real all time points |language=english}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Unsung heroes: Jody Scheckter |url=https://www.ferrari.com/magazine/articles/unsung-heroes-jody-scheckter |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.ferrari.com |language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Sport |first=Motor |date=2025-06-20 |title='It was like a mafia scene' - Scheckter recalls Ferrari meeting that shaped 1979 F1 season |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/articles/single-seaters/f1/it-was-like-a-mafia-scene-scheckter-recalls-ferrari-meeting-that-shaped-1979-f1-season/ |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB}}</ref>

==== 1979-80: Championship and retirement ====
[[File:Jody Scheckter 1979 Monaco.jpg|thumb|left|Scheckter driving for [[Scuderia Ferrari|Ferrari]] at the [[1979 Monaco Grand Prix]].]]Scheckter's [[1979 Formula One season|1979]] began badly, as he sprained his wrist in a lap 1 collision with John Watson.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Argentine GP, 1979 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/races/argentine-gp-1979.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> After pitting for new tyres, Scheckter finished sixth in [[1979 Brazilian Grand Prix|Brazil]], the last race of the [[Ferrari 312T#312T3|Ferrari 312T3]] chassis.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Brazilian GP, 1979 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr315.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> Once the [[Ferrari 312T#312T4|312T4]] was introduced in [[1979 South African Grand Prix|South Africa]], Ferrari scored a 1-2, with Villeneuve leading home Scheckter.<ref>{{Cite web |title=South African GP, 1979 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr316.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> The pair achieved the same result in [[1979 United States Grand Prix West|Long Beach]], before Villeneuve spun in [[1979 Spanish Grand Prix|Spain]] and Scheckter finished fourth.<ref>{{Cite web |title=United States GP, 1979 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr317.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Spanish GP, 1979 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr318.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> In a charging drive from seventh on the grid, Scheckter claimed his first win of the season at [[1979 Belgian Grand Prix|Zolder]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Belgian GP, 1979 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr319.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> He then qualified on pole for the [[1979 Monaco Grand Prix|Monaco Grand Prix]] and staved off a challenge from [[Clay Regazzoni]] to take victory.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Jenkinson |first=Denis |date=2016-01-22 |title=1979 Monaco Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/july-1979/63/monaco-grand-prix-8/ |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB}}</ref> Seventh in [[1979 French Grand Prix|France]] preceded a fifth place in [[1979 British Grand Prix|Britain]] and fourth in [[1979 German Grand Prix|Germany]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=French GP, 1979 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr321.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=British GP, 1979 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr322.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=German GP, 1979 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr323.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> Scheckter finished fourth again in [[1979 Austrian Grand Prix|Austria]], before recovering from a bad start in [[1979 Dutch Grand Prix|Zandvoort]] to charge up to second.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Austrian GP, 1979 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr324.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Dutch GP, 1979 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr325.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref> With a popular win at Ferrari's home in [[1979 Italian Grand Prix|Monza]], Scheckter clinched the world championship two races in advance.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Italian GP, 1979 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr326.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Jenkinson |first=Denis |date=2014-07-07 |title=1979 Italian Grand Prix race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/october-1979/39/italian-grand-prix-5/ |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB}}</ref><ref name="hof">{{cite web|url=http://www.formula1.com/teams_and_drivers/hall_of_fame/283/|publisher=Fédération Internationale de l'Automobile|title=F1 Teams & Drivers Hall of Fame: Jody Schekter|archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20071128233824/http://www.formula1.com/teams_and_drivers/hall_of_fame/283/ |access-date=24 October 2007 |archive-date= 28 November 2007 |url-status= dead}}</ref> He concluded the year with a fourth place in [[1979 Canadian Grand Prix|Canada]] and a retirement caused by tyre failure in the [[1979 United States Grand Prix|United States]].<ref>{{Cite web |title=Canadian GP, 1979 |url=https://www.grandprix.com/gpe/rr327.html |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.grandprix.com}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |last=Jenkinson |first=Denis |date=2014-07-07 |title=1979 United States Grand Prix (East) race report |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/november-1979/39/united-states-east-grand-prix-2/ |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=Motor Sport Magazine |language=en-GB}}</ref> 

However, Scheckter struggled badly in his [[1980 Formula One season|1980]] title defence, even failing to qualify for the [[1980 Canadian Grand Prix|Canadian Grand Prix]]. After managing only two points, he announced his retirement from the team and the sport. He later explained his retirement by stating: "One or two drivers were dying every year, I’d won the championship and the magic was gone."<ref>{{Cite web |title=A life well lived |url=https://www.ferrari.com/magazine/articles/a-life-well-lived-jody-sheckter |access-date=2025-12-29 |website=www.ferrari.com |language=en}}</ref>

Scheckter was the last driver to win a Drivers' Championship for Ferrari until [[Michael Schumacher]] twenty-one years later in {{F1|2000}}.

==Other ventures==
===Broadcasting===
{{BLP unsourced section|date=September 2024}}
In 1981, [[CBS Sports]] hired Scheckter as a Pit reporter for its [[F1]] coverage. Scheckter was brought in by [[ABC Sports|ABC's]] [[Wide World of Sports (American TV program)|''Wide World of Sports'']] as a Pit reporter for the [[1983 Monaco Grand Prix]]. Scheckter was a guest commentator for ITV during the [[1999 San Marino Grand Prix]], replacing [[Martin Brundle]].

Scheckter won the [[Superstars (British TV programme)#World Superstars|World Superstars]] competition in 1981, taking place in [[Key Biscayne, Florida]]. He defeated athletes such as [[Russ Francis]], [[Renaldo Nehemiah]], [[Peter Müller (skier)|Peter Müller]], [[Rick Barry]], [[Gaétan Boucher]] and [[Andy Ripley]].

===Business===

After his retirement, Scheckter founded a [[Military simulation|weapons simulation]] business in 1984 called Firearms Training Systems (FATS), whose [[revenue]] eclipsed £100 million by the early 1990s.<ref name="FATS">{{cite journal |last1=Smith |first1=Damien |title=Jody Scheckter – And he walked away |journal=[[Motor Sport (magazine)|Motor Sport]] |date=February 2013 |pages=66–70 |url=https://www.motorsportmagazine.com/archive/article/february-2013/66/jody-scheckter-and-he-walked-away |access-date=23 May 2020}}</ref> The company built firearms training simulators for military, law enforcement and security organisations.<ref name="FATS"/>

===Farming===
Scheckter spent his time as a [[Biodynamic agriculture|biodynamic farmer]],<ref>{{cite news|title=Formula One's Jody Scheckter Turns to Meat|publisher=foodmanufacture.co.uk|url=http://www.foodmanufacture.co.uk/news/fullstory.php/aid/4388/Formula_One%27s_Jody_Scheckter_turns_to_meat.html|date=1 March 2007|url-status=dead|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071223001440/http://www.foodmanufacture.co.uk/news/fullstory.php/aid/4388/Formula_One%27s_Jody_Scheckter_turns_to_meat.html|archive-date=23 December 2007|access-date=16 October 2007}}</ref> having bought the {{convert|2500|acre}}<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=http://www.independent.co.uk/news/people/profiles/jody-scheckter-from-formula-one-to-life-in-the-slow-lane-2070839.html |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/archive/20220609/http://www.independent.co.uk/news/people/profiles/jody-scheckter-from-formula-one-to-life-in-the-slow-lane-2070839.html |archive-date=9 June 2022 |url-access=subscription |url-status=live|title=Jody Scheckter: From Formula One to life in the slow lane|date=2010-09-05|website=The Independent|language=en|access-date=2019-08-28}}</ref> [[Laverstoke Park Farm]], near [[Overton, Hampshire]], {{convert|40|mi|km|0}} south west of London. As an organic farming expert, Scheckter was featured in 2005 on the Visionhealth DVD and TV documentaries "[[Asthma]]: An Integrated Approach", "[[Arthritis]]: An Integrated Approach" and "[[Diabetes]]: An Integrated Approach". On 20 November 2011, he also appeared on the ''[[Countryfile]]'' television show to make a case for [[organic food]].  Laverstoke Park Farm was also featured on BBC's ''Escape To the Country'' where Scheckter showed viewers how [[Buffalo Mozzarella]] was made.  In December 2009, Scheckter announced his intention to produce a [[Biodynamic agriculture|biodynamic]] [[sparkling wine]] by 2012.<ref>Lawrence, James, ''Decanter.com'' (14 December 2009). [http://www.decanter.com/news/292915.html Jody Scheckter announces Hampshire winery plans] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20091217103505/http://www.decanter.com/news/292915.html |date=17 December 2009 }}</ref> In 2015, the farm was the setting for ITV's ''Sugar Free Farm'' where a group of celebrities had to go sugar free for two weeks whilst working on the farm.
In 2024, the farm ceased dairy production and buffalo farming with operations at the compost plant not affected and the farm would continue to host [[CarFest|carfest]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Company Announcement 1st January 2024|url=https://www.laverstokepark.co.uk/|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20240121060739/https://www.laverstokepark.co.uk/|archive-date=2024-01-21|date=2024-01-01}}</ref>

===Charity===
In 2004, Scheckter was reunited with his championship-winning Ferrari at the South African two-seater F1x2 Charity Grand Prix at [[Kyalami]] in South Africa.

==Personal life==

Scheckter is married and has had six children: two, [[Toby Scheckter|Toby]] and [[Tomas Scheckter|Tomas]], from his previous wife Pamela; and four, Hugo, Freddie, Ila and Poppy, from his current marriage to Clare.

Scheckter's daughter Ila died on 17 October 2019, at age 21.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.basingstokegazette.co.uk/news/17983842.daughter-formula-1-champion-jody-scheckter-dies-aged-21-suspected-accidental-overdose/|title=Daughter of Formula One champion Jody Scheckter dies aged 21 after 'suspected accidental overdose'|website=Basingstoke Gazette|date=21 October 2019 }}</ref> The sale of his firearms simulation company provided funds to allow Scheckter to help the racing careers of his sons Tomas and Toby. Tomas raced in the [[Indy Racing League]] where he won two races. Scheckter's elder brother [[Ian Scheckter|Ian]] also raced in Formula One.

==Awards and honours==
In 1983, he was inducted into the [[International Jewish Sports Hall of Fame]].

In 2024, at the [[South African Jewish Board of Deputies]]' 120th anniversary gala dinner, he was honoured among 100 remarkable Jewish South Africans who have contributed to South Africa.<ref name=gala>(28 November 2024) [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3jorzdQVRhI 100 Jews who have contributed to South Africa] SABJD. Retrieved on 1 February 2026.</ref> The ceremony included speeches from Chief Rabbi [[Ephraim Mirvis]], and Scheckter was honoured among other sports figures such as [[Ali Bacher]] and [[Joel Stransky]].<ref name=gala/><ref>[https://www.sajr.co.za/story-of-tenacity-board-honours-great-south-africans/ Story of tenacity: Board honours great South Africans] ''South African Jewish Report''. Retrieved on 1 February 2026.</ref>
